<answer>
The current tallest wooden lattice tower was completed in **2019**. This title belongs to Mjøstårnet in Brumunddal, Norway, which stands at 85.4 meters (280 feet) and is recognized globally as the tallest all-wood building as well as a notable example of contemporary timber architecture.

## Understanding "Wooden Lattice Tower"

A "lattice tower" typically refers to a tower composed of an intersecting framework of metal or wood members that form a rigid and stable structure. Traditionally, these towers were made from steel, such as the iconic Eiffel Tower in Paris. However, advancements in timber technology and a growing emphasis on sustainable construction have led to innovative uses of wood in architectural forms that resemble lattice structures.

In the case of Mjøstårnet, while the design incorporates modern cross-laminated timber (CLT) and glued laminated timber (glulam) elements—rather than a traditional open lattice—the overall shape and internal support system give it the characteristic feel of a lattice tower. Its use of prefabricated timber panels and exposed structural beams aligns visually and structurally with the concept of a lattice tower constructed from wood.

## Overview of Mjøstårnet

Mjøstårnet represents a milestone in sustainable urban development. Here are key details:

| Attribute                  | Details                                 |
|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Location                   | Brumunddal, Norway                      |
| Height                     | 85.4 meters (280 feet)                  |
| Completion Year            | 2019                                    |
| Architectural Style        | Contemporary Timber (noted for CLT/glulam) |
| Uses                       | Residential apartments, hotel, offices  |
| Significance               | Tallest all-wood building worldwide     |

Construction began in 2017, and after two years of intensive building, the tower opened its doors in 2019. It set a new benchmark for how timber can be engineered into multifunctional high-rise buildings, supporting both environmental sustainability and aesthetic appeal.

## Historical Context

For many years, the tallest known wooden towers were limited by material constraints and technological limitations. Early wooden lattice towers often served practical purposes, such as fire lookout posts or radio transmission masts, but rarely exceeded 50 meters in height. In recent decades, renewed interest in timber as a renewable building material has fostered innovations in composite wood products and modular construction methods.

Mjøstårnet exemplifies these advances. Its success has inspired similar projects in other countries, pushing the boundaries of what is possible with timber construction. While there are plans for even taller wooden towers—such as HoHo Tower in Vienna, Austria—it is Mjøstårnet that currently holds the record.

## Sustainability and Engineering

Timber-based construction has become increasingly popular due to its environmental benefits. Wood absorbs carbon dioxide during growth and stores it throughout the building’s lifespan, reducing the carbon footprint compared to concrete and steel alternatives. The engineering behind Mjøstårnet demonstrates that large-scale structures can be both strong and environmentally responsible.

The building's construction utilized advanced techniques like:
- Prefabrication to reduce on-site waste
- Use of certified wood sourced from sustainable forests
- Fire-resistant coatings and designs to meet stringent safety standards

This ensures that wood can be used not only for historical or decorative elements but also as a primary structural material in ambitious projects.

## Comparison with Other Wooden Towers

To put Mjøstårnet’s achievements in context, here’s a brief overview of other notable tall wooden buildings completed around the same period:

| Name            | Location           | Height      | Completion Year | Primary Use                |
|-----------------|--------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Mjøstårnet      | Brumunddal, Norway | 85.4 m      | 2019            | Residential/hotel/offices  |
| Oakwood Tower   | Berlin, Germany    | 73 m        | 2021            | Residential                |
| Ascent          | Zurich, Switzerland| 73 m        | 2020            | Residential                |

While each of these projects showcases the versatility and strength of modern timber engineering, Mjøstårnet remains the tallest as of now.

## Conclusion

In summary, the current tallest wooden lattice tower—Mjøstårnet—was completed in **2019**. Located in Brumunddal, Norway, it stands at 85.4 meters and marks a significant achievement in sustainable architecture. Its completion reflects ongoing trends toward green building practices and the creative repurposing of traditional materials through modern engineering. As the push for eco-friendly construction intensifies, it is likely that even taller wooden towers will soon join the ranks of timber marvels like Mjøstårnet.</answer>

However, advancements in timber technology and a growing emphasis on sustainable construction have led to innovative uses of wood in architectural forms that resemble lattice structures.

In the case of Mjøstårnet, while the design incorporates modern cross-laminated timber (CLT) and glued laminated timber (glulam) elements—rather than a traditional open lattice—the overall shape and internal support system give it the characteristic feel of a lattice tower. Its use of prefabricated timber panels and exposed structural beams aligns visually and structurally with the concept of a lattice tower constructed from wood.

## Overview of Mjøstårnet

Mjøstårnet represents a milestone in sustainable urban development. Here are key details:

| Attribute                  | Details                                 |
|----------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Location                   | Brumunddal, Norway                      |
| Height                     | 85.4 meters (280 feet)                  |
| Completion Year            | 2019                                    |
| Architectural Style        | Contemporary Timber (noted for CLT/glulam) |
| Uses                       | Residential apartments, hotel, offices  |
| Significance               | Tallest all-wood building worldwide     |

Construction began in 2017, and after two years of intensive building, the tower opened its doors in 2019. It set a new benchmark for how timber can be engineered into multifunctional high-rise buildings, supporting both environmental sustainability and aesthetic appeal.

## Historical Context

For many years, the tallest known wooden towers were limited by material constraints and technological limitations. Early wooden lattice towers often served practical purposes, such as fire lookout posts or radio transmission masts, but rarely exceeded 50 meters in height. In recent decades, renewed interest in timber as a renewable building material has fostered innovations in composite wood products and modular construction methods.

Mjøstårnet exemplifies these advances. Its success has inspired similar projects in other countries, pushing the boundaries of what is possible with timber construction. While there are plans for even taller wooden towers—such as HoHo Tower in Vienna, Austria—it is Mjøstårnet that currently holds the record.

## Sustainability and Engineering

Timber-based construction has become increasingly popular due to its environmental benefits. Wood absorbs carbon dioxide during growth and stores it throughout the building’s lifespan, reducing the carbon footprint compared to concrete and steel alternatives. The engineering behind Mjøstårnet demonstrates that large-scale structures can be both strong and environmentally responsible.

The building's construction utilized advanced techniques like:
- Prefabrication to reduce on-site waste
- Use of certified wood sourced from sustainable forests
- Fire-resistant coatings and designs to meet stringent safety standards

This ensures that wood can be used not only for historical or decorative elements but also as a primary structural material in ambitious projects.

## Comparison with Other Wooden Towers

To put Mjøstårnet’s achievements in context, here’s a brief overview of other notable tall wooden buildings completed around the same period:

| Name            | Location           | Height      | Completion Year | Primary Use                |
|-----------------|--------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Mjøstårnet      | Brumunddal, Norway | 85.4 m      | 2019            | Residential/hotel/offices  |
| Oakwood Tower   | Berlin, Germany    | 73 m        | 2021            | Residential                |
| Ascent          | Zurich, Switzerland| 73 m        | 2020            | Residential                |

While each of these projects showcases the versatility and strength of modern timber engineering, Mjøstårnet remains the tallest as of now.

## Conclusion

In summary, the current tallest wooden lattice tower—Mjøstårnet—was completed in **2019**. Located in Brumunddal, Norway, it stands at 85.4 meters and marks a significant achievement in sustainable architecture. Its completion reflects ongoing trends toward green building practices and the creative repurposing of traditional materials through modern engineering. As the push for eco-friendly construction intensifies, it is likely that even taller wooden towers will soon join the ranks of timber marvels like Mjøstårnet.
